Daiwa House Industry Co., Ltd. Revises Consolidated Earnings Guidance for the Fiscal Year Ending March 31, 2026
data.description:
Daiwa House Industry Co., Ltd. revised consolidated earnings guidance for the fiscal year ending March 31, 2026. For the year, the company now expects net sales of JPY 5,600,000 million compared to previous guidance of JPY 5,600,000 million, operating income of JPY 510,000 million compared to previous guidance of JPY 470,000 million and net income attributable to owners of the parent of JPY 290,000 million or JPY 468.74 per basic share compared to previous guidance of JPY 273,000 million or JPY 441.33 per basic share. Reasons for the Revision of Consolidated Earnings: In the 7th Medium-Term Management Plan, a five-year plan that began in fiscal 2022, the Company is pursuing corporate activities based on the three management policies of "evolving revenue model," "Optimizing management efficiency," and "strengthening the management base" in order to realize a sustainable growth model.

While progress in the first six-month period was on track with the plan, a large-scale land sale transaction took place at the Company's U.S. subsidiary at the end of October 2025. In addition, after carefully reviewing the schedules for real estate sales in each business segment, the Company now expects results to exceed the consolidated earnings forecasts announced in May 2025. Therefore, the Company has decided to revise its consolidated earnings forecasts for the fiscal year ending March 2026.

The Company's basic strategy for capital policy is to achieve ROE that exceeds the cost of capital while maintaining financial stability, and to secure equity spreads to increase shareholder value. The Company's basic policy on shareholder returns is to return profits generated through business activities to shareholders, and to increase shareholder value by increasing its earnings per share (EPS) with investing in growth areas such as real estate development, the expansion of overseas business, M&A, research and development, and production facilities in order to maximize corporate value over the medium to long term.

data.title: Daiwa House Industry Co., Ltd. Announces Dividend for the Second Quarter Ended September 30, 2025, Payable on December 5, 2025 and Provides Dividend Guidance for the Year Ending March 31, 2026
data.description:
Daiwa House Industry Co., Ltd. announced dividend of JPY 75.00 per share for the second quarter ended September 30, 2025 compared to JPY 70.00 per share paid a year ago. Scheduled Date of Commencement of Dividend Payment: December 5, 2025.

The company provided dividend guidance for the year ending March 31, 2026. The company expects to pay year end dividend of JPY 90.00 per share for the year ending March 31, 2026 compared to JPY 80.00 per share paid a year ago.

data.title: Yokohama Financial Group, Inc. Revises Earnings Guidance for the Fiscal Year Ending March 31, 2026
data.description:
Yokohama Financial Group, Inc. revised consolidated earnings guidance for the fiscal year ending March 31, 2026. For the period, the company expects profit attributable to owners of parent to be JPY 103,000 million compared to previous guidance of JPY 95,500 million and net income per share to be JPY 90.64 compared to previous guidance of JPY 83.66 per share. Reason for the revision: In banking subsidiaries, net interest income and net fees and commissions are expected to increase.

Accordingly, the Company has revised upward the previously announced consolidated earnings forecast for the fiscal year ending March 31, 2026.

data.title: Applied Materials forecasts strong quarterly revenue on AI chip demand
data.description:
(Reuters) -Applied Materials, U.S.-based maker of semiconductor manufacturing equipment, forecast upbeat first-quarter revenue on Thursday, signaling that robust demand for tools used to make AI chips is set to overpower a broader industry slowdown.

The company forecast current-quarter revenue of $6.85 billion, plus or minus $500 million. Analysts on average expect revenue of $6.76 billion, according to data compiled by LSEG.

(Reporting by Akash Sriram in Bengaluru; Editing by Tasim Zahid)

Siemens Energy Boosts Long-Term Outlook
data.description:
By Katherine Hamilton


Siemens Energy raised its long-term outlook.

The energy-technology solutions company raised its target for profit margin in fiscal-year 2028 for 14% to 16% before special items.

It increased its outlook for comparable compound annual revenue growth to a low-teen percentage range until fiscal-year 2028, from high-single digits to low- double digits previously.

The Munich company said it expects adjusted revenue in the current fiscal year to grow 11% to 13%.


Write to Katherine Hamilton at katherine.hamilton@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

11-13-25 1558ET

data.title: Siemens Energy Raises Mid-Term Targets Again After Profit Surge
data.description:
MUNICH (dpa-AFX) - Energy technology group Siemens Energy continues to experience a boom. Business with gas turbines is thriving, as is grid technology. As a result, following a surge in revenue and profit, the company has once again raised its mid-term targets through 2027/28.

The company announced in Munich on Thursday that comparable revenue is now expected to grow annually at a high double-digit percentage rate on average during this period. Previously, CEO Christian Bruch and his management team had projected growth in the high single-digit to low double-digit percentage range. Currency and portfolio effects are excluded from these figures. The adjusted profit margin is now forecast to reach 14 to 16 percent, compared to the previous target of 10 to 12 percent. The new forecast exceeds analysts' previous expectations, who had anticipated an average margin of 13.5 percent.

Shares jumped 3.3 percent on the Tradegate trading platform after having closed deep in the red on the Xetra exchange./nas/he

Corn Futures Higher After USDA Starts Releasing Export Sales -- Daily Grain Highlights
data.description:
By Kirk Maltais


-- Corn for December delivery rose 1.6% to $4.42 1/4 a bushel on the Chicago Board of Trade on Thursday, this after the USDA released export sales reports for the last two weeks of September, and also its schedule for export sales reports missed during the government shutdown.

-- Soybeans for January delivery rose 1.1% to $11.46 a bushel.

-- Wheat for December delivery fell 0.1% to $5.35 1/4 a bushel.


HIGHLIGHTS


Back to Business: The break ended for U.S. grain export sales Thursday. Some missed reports surfaced this morning, the first new export sales data since the end of the government shutdown, for the last two weeks of September.

This week's report showed data for the weeks ended Sept. 18 and Sept. 25. The USDA reported total corn sales were 1.92 million metric tons for Sept. 18, soybean sales were 724,500 tons and wheat sales were 539,900 tons.

For the week ended Sept. 25, corn sales were 1.42 million tons, soybean sales were 870,500 tons and wheat sales were 315,900 tons.

No mention of Chinese buying was seen in the report.


Blame It on the Rain: CBOT wheat futures couldn't manage to get the support seen in corn and soybeans, and were under pressure as global cash prices see weakness, according to AgResource in a note.

The firm said Friday's WASDE report is expected to make cuts to previously forecast corn and soybean yields, fueling upticks in both.

"The market has a large amount of 'yield worry' built into the price," said AgResource.


INSIGHT


Looking for the Evidence: The USDA said this morning that it will be publishing its daily sales of grains to private exporters that wasn't reported during the 43-day government shutdown.

Traders will be watching for this release, in hopes that the USDA says China was a customer for soybeans anytime from October to early November.

"We better see China's name on that report," said Brian Pullam of Linn & Associates.

Absence of China would likely introduce selling pressure to CBOT grains, particularly soybeans.


Setting the Scene: The WASDE report may serve as a barometer for how much stock the agency is putting in the ongoing U.S.-China negotiations, and the hopes of increased soybean export sales.

"If the USDA cuts it it tells me they do not have much faith in our 'trade deals' we are making," said Karl Setzer of Consus Ag Consulting.

The USDA will publish the sales made to private exporters Friday, at the same time as the WASDE release.


Big Piles: Brazilian agricultural agency Conab estimates the total 2025-26 Brazilian grain harvest at 354.8 million metric tons.

Soybean production is estimated at 177.6 million tons, and corn production is seen at 138.8 million tons.

Conab also published its outlook for how planting for the next crop fared. Weather for soybean planting was seen as too dry in states such as Goiás, Minas Gerais and Mato Grosso. Low temperatures in October are also affecting freshly planted corn.


AHEAD


-- The USDA is scheduled to release its November WASDE report on Friday, rescheduled from 12 p.m. EST on Nov. 10 because of the government shutdown.

-- The USDA is due to release the backlog of daily export sales left unreported during the government shutdown at 12 p.m. EST on Friday.

-- The USDA is scheduled to release its monthly Cattle on Feed Report at 3 p.m. EST on Nov. 21.


Write to Kirk Maltais at kirk.maltais@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

11-13-25 1548ET

BBC apologises to Trump over speech edit, rejects compensation claim
data.description:
LONDON (Reuters) -Britain's BBC apologised to Donald Trump on Thursday for editing a speech to make it look like he had advocated violence but rejected the president's demand for compensation.

The broadcaster also said in a statement it would not show the programme again.

"While the BBC sincerely regrets the manner in which the video clip was edited, we strongly disagree there is a basis for a defamation claim," the BBC said.

(Reporting by Catarina Demony)

Grand Rapids to Issue $175 Million Bond to Fund Senior Living Expansion
data.description:
By Paulo Trevisani


The Economic Development Corporation of the City of Grand Rapids, Mich., plans to issue $174.83 million in bonds to finance the expansion of the Beacon Hill at Eastgate senior living community.

The proceeds will fund the demolition, acquisition, construction, furnishing, and equipping of new senior housing and healthcare facilities, while also covering other obligations, according to a preliminary official statement published Thursday on MuniOs.

The expansion will include 101 new independent living units and 40 assisted living units, while also renovating existing healthcare facilities.

The Limited Obligation Revenue Bonds are tax-exempt and will have no coupons.

The issuance is divided into four tranches. The Series 2025A Fixed Rate Bonds total $107.83 million and mature through 2060. They carry assumed interest rates ranging from 5.825% to 6.1% a year.

Series 2025B-1 TEMPS-85SM amounts to $12.25 million, Series 2025B-2 TEMPS-70SM to $15.75 million and Series 2025B-3 TEMPS-50SM totals $39 million. They all mature in 2030 and carry an assumed interest rate of 4.625% a year.

The bonds are payable from the revenues and funds pledged by the Michigan Christian Home Obligated Group.

The Obligor Group's total revenue, gains and other support for the fiscal year ended March 31 was $21.1 million.

The bonds are not rated. The underwriter is Ziegler.


Write to Paulo Trevisani at paulo.trevisani@wsj.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

11-13-25 1529ET

data.title: Apple lowers App Store commissions for 'mini apps'
data.description:
SAN FRANCISCO (Reuters) -Apple on Thursday said it would lower commissions for "mini apps" on iPhones and other devices.

Apple's App Store charges commissions of up to 30% on digital purchases. Under the program announced Thursday, that rate will come down to 15% for developers of mini apps - often game titles and other apps that are offered inside a larger "host app" - if they adopt certain Apple technologies such as its method for declaring an age range of the user.

Mini apps are most common in China, where popular apps like Tencent Holdings and Alipay host scores of game titles or other services inside their primary application. But the model is also being adopted by U.S. firms, with ChatGPT creator OpenAI last month saying it will offer mini apps within its flagship chatbot app.

Under Apple's App Store rules, developers of mini apps pay commissions for digital sales directly to Apple. How much of a cut the host app takes remains between the host app and the mini app.

Apple's linking of the lower prices to its age declaration technology comes as the iPhone maker is in a dispute with states and tech rivals such as Meta Platforms over age verification on digital platforms.

Multiple U.S. states are pursuing laws that would require verifying the age of app users, and Meta has said it believes the best place for that verification to happen is at the level of the app marketplaces run by Alphabet's Google and Apple.

Apple has argued that the laws would require it to invade the privacy of adult users and has proposed an alternative that would allow app developers to accept an age range that users declare, which would need to be approved by an adult in the case of younger users.

(Reporting by Stephen Nellis in San Francisco; Editing by Cynthia Osterman)

By Stephen Nellis
